children.name: Hemiprocne
children.description: Elegant, long-winged swifts with a distinctive crest and a forked tail, differing from true swifts in their habit of perching conspicuously in trees. They are among the most graceful aerialists of the Asian and Australasian tropics.

children.name: Hemiprocne coronata
children.description: Found from the Indian subcontinent through Southeast Asia, this pale grey treeswift with a blue-black crown and crest hunts insects in long, sweeping forays above the forest canopy. Unlike true swifts, it perches on exposed branches to rest and preen between feeding flights. The crest is more prominent in males, especially during the breeding season.

children.name: Hemiprocne mystacea
children.description: The largest treeswift, distinguished by its bold white whisker-like cheek stripe and glossy blue-black upperparts. It occurs from the Moluccas and New Guinea to the Solomon Islands. Its swift, graceful flight is accented by a deeply forked tail, and it often glides in wide circles high above the rainforest.

children.name: Hemiprocne longipennis
children.description: A medium-sized treeswift with a greyish rump contrasting with glossy dark upperparts, ranging from Myanmar through the Malay Peninsula to Sumatra and Borneo. It is frequently seen hawking for insects above forest clearings and plantations. Its call is a high-pitched, twittering series of notes given in flight.
